children.title: Recursion Basics
children.description: Recursion is the core mechanism behind divide and conquer. It allows a function to call itself on smaller sub‑problems until a simple base case is reached.

children.title: Problem Partitioning
children.description: The original problem is split into independent sub‑problems that are easier to solve. Effective partitioning reduces the overall complexity and enables parallel processing.

children.title: Combine Step Strategies
children.description: After solving sub‑problems, their results must be merged to form the final answer. The combine step often determines the overall efficiency of the algorithm.

children.title: Recurrence Relations
children.description: Recurrences express the running time of divide and conquer algorithms in terms of sub‑problem sizes. Solving them (e.g., via the Master Theorem) reveals the algorithm's asymptotic complexity.

children.title: Common Divide and Conquer Algorithms
children.description: Classic examples include Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, and Fast Fourier Transform. Studying these illustrates how the pattern adapts to different problem domains.
